Farn Polrrrcel PnncrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
November 7,2003
Kathryn Lyddan, Executive Director
Brentwood Agricultur al Land Trust
104 Oak Street
Brentwood, CA 94513-1396
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-03-182
Dear Ms. Lydd,art:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Brentwood
Agricultural Land Trust and Tom Bloomfield, regarding the conflict of interest and
conflict of interest code provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTIONS
l. 
Are the members of the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust Board of
Directors and its executive director subject to the requiranents of the Act?
2. 
If the Brentwood AgriculturalLatdTrust is subject to the Act, is it
required to adopt and implement its own conflict of interest code, or should it comply
with the conflict of interest code of another agency?
3. 
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act and should
comply under the conflict of interest code of another agency, what public agency is its
appropriate fi ling offi cer?
4. 
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, does the
consulting and farm work done by Boardmember Tom Bloomfield for the property owner
with whom the trust is currently negotiating for a conservation easement give rise to a
conflict of interest under the Act that would preclude Mr. Bloomfield from participating
in board discussions and decisions pertaining to a conservation easement on the property?
lGovemmentCodesectionsSl000-91014. Commissionregulations appeffatTitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
-ua-dl


FileNo. A-03-182
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. 
In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth four criteria to consider in
determining whether an entity should be considered a "local govemment agenct''subject
to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we find that the Brentwood Agricultural Trust
meets all four criteria and, therefore, conclude that it is a local government agency under
section 82041.
2. 
Because we have concluded that the Brentwood Agriculfural Land Trust is a local
government agerrcy subject to the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest code or
to be covered by the code of another agency. Since it covers more than one jurisdiction
(both the City of Brentwood and part of Contra Costa County), the code reviewing body
for the trust is the board of supervisors. The board of supervisors must make the ultimate
determination as to whether the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust must adopt its own
conflict of interest code or be subsumed within the county's code
3. 
Pursuant to section 87500(o), BALT's board of directors and its executive
director would file with the agency or with the code reviewing body, as provided by the
board of supervisors, the code reviewing body.
4. 
Yes. Because the property owner is a source of income equal to or more than
$500 to Mr. Bloomfield within 12 months of a govemmental decision that directly
involves the property owner, Mr. Bloomfield has a conflict of interest and may not
participate in the decision.
FACTS
On August 28,2001, a task force formed by the City of Brentwood issued the
Agricultural Enterprise Program Final Report. The report recommended certain steps
that the city could take to preserve agricultural land and promote agricultural enterprise in
and around the city. On the same date, the Brentwood City Council adopted Ordinance
No. 683 implementing the Agricultural Enterprise Program described in the report and
adding certain provisions to the city's municipal code. Pursuant to the ordinance,
developers submitting subdivision applications for discretionary land use entitlements
from the city must provide agricultural land mitigation by either: (i) granting a farmland
conservation easement to a qualifying entity on a one to one land area ratio, or (ii) paying
an in lieu fee to fund the purchase of such an easement. The ordinance also provides thai
a local land trust should be formed to acquire and hold such agricultural conservation
easements.
on october 24,2002, the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust ("BALT') was
formed pursuant to the Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation Law of California. The
Articles of Incorporation were signed by the board of directors. The bylaws of BALT
provide that BALT shall implement the Agricultural Enterprise Program described in the
ordinance and work to preserve agricultural land in the city and east Contra Costa
County. BALT is actively seeking to acquire agricultural conservation easements in east


File No. A-03-182
Page No. 3
Contra Costa County. An agricultural conservation easernent is arealproperty interest
recorded against farmland that limits the land to agricultural uses in perpetuity. In
exchange for granting a conservation easement to BALT, a property owner can receive
compensation for the development value of the land and significant tax benefits. It is
anticipated that BALT will accept donated conservation easements and purchase
easements from property owners. The purchase of conservation easements will be
funded through the mitigation fees levied by the city as well as state Department of
Conservation and federal matching funds.
Composition of the BALT Board of Directors. Thereport recommends a method
for appointing the BALT Board of Directors, and that recommendation is reflected in the
bylaws of BALT. Pursuant to Section 3 of the bylaws, BALT has a seven-member board
of directors. Three of the directors are appointed by the city, three of the directors are
appointed by the East Contra Costa Irrigation District ("ECCID") and one director is
appointed by the other six. ECCID is a Califomia public agency established pursuant to
the Irrigation District Law of the Califomia Water Code.' The geographical jurisdiction
of ECCID is greater than the city but within Contra Costa County. ECCID files the Form
700 of its employees and board of directors with the county.
Executive Dtrector of BALT. In June 2003,BALT hired an executive director to
work eighty hours a month. While BALT has its own post office box and pays the city
for any city services it uses, the executive director currently has an office in the
community development departrnent of the city.
In a telephone conversation on October 21,2003, with Commission staff, you
explained the following: the city does not charge BALT for rent on the office space it
occupies, and the city purchased BALT's telephone and computer system. The city,s
Ordinance No. 683 provides that of the $5,500 per acre agricultural mitigation fee ievied
by the city on developers ,20yo of those funds may be used for BALT's administrative
costs. The city holds the mitigation fees collected in two separate bank accounts, one for
conservation easements and one for BALT's administrative expenses. Once each quarter,
BALT submits a check request to the city for one-fourth of BALT's annual operating
expenses. BALT's annual budget is approved by both the BALT Board of Directors and
by the city council. Although no conservation easements have yet been purchased by
BALT, it is anticipated that when such purchases are made by the board, the item will be
placed on the city council agenda to approve the release of the funds from the mitigation
fee account to complete the purchase.
You also confirmed that BALT complies with the open meeting provisions of the
Brown Act.
Potenttal Confltct of Interest. In the spring of 2003,BALT began negotiations
with a local property owner interested in recording an agricultural conservation easement
2 krigation diskicts are state agencies pursuant to Water Code $ 2}57},but are treated as local
government agencies for puqposes of the Act. (See Francis Advice Letter, No. 4-00-23 1.) Because the
ECCID covers only one count5r, the board of supervisors is its code reviewing body. (Section 82011(b).)


FileNo. A-03-182
Page No. 4
on his property. Twenty-five acres of the eighty-acre property is cultivated in grapes.
The property owner has told the BALT Board of Directors that he plans to spend iome of
the moneys he obtains from the sale of the conservation easement to plant grapes on
some or all of the remaining 55 acres of the property. However, it is not anticipated that
BALT will condition the purchase of the conservation easement on any particular use of
the purchase price by the property owner. Assuming that the negotiations between BALT
and the property owner continue successfully, it is likely that it will take six to twelve
months before the conservation easement can be recorded against the property.
One of the members of the BALT Board of Directors, whom you identified to
Commission staff as Tom Bloomfield, is a corlmercial gtapeproperty owner. He has
provided consulting and grape farming services to the property owner regarding the
cultivation of the property. Mr. Bloomfield has received income in excess of $500 within
the past twelve months from the property owner as compensation for rendering such
consulting and farming services.
ANALYSIS
Ouestion l. 
Are the members of the Brentwood Agriculfural Land Trust Board of
Directors and its executive director subject to the requirements of the
Act?
A. Applicable Law.
A "local govemment agency''is defined in the Act as "a county, city or district of
any kind including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or
any departrnent, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." (Section 82041.)
The inquiry here is whether BALT is a local government agency. The
Commission used a four-part factual test to distinguish govemmental from non-
governmental entities in In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62. In the Siegel opinion, the
Commission was faced with the question of whether the Pico Rivera Water Development
Corporation, a nonprofit corporation, should be considered a local government entiiy.
The Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation was a nonprofit corporation that was
founded to acquire, maintain, and operate a water system.
To determine whether the Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation was a
govemmental entity, the Commission considered four questions:
(1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity
originated with a govemment agency;
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its
primary source of funds is, a government agency;


FileNo. A-03-182
Page No. 5
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the
entity was formed is to provide services or undertake
obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to
perform and which, infact, they traditionallyhave
performed; and
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other
statutory provisions.
The Commission found that the city council was directly involved in the
formation of the water development corporation, and that the city council had the right to
disapprove the name of anyone submitted to serve on the board. With respect to funding,
it found that the city was required to pay rent to the corporation until the bonds were
retired, even if receipts from the operation of the water system were not sufficient to meet
these costs - in essence, guaranteeing the bonds of the corporation. More evidence that
the corporation was fulfilling a public function was the factthatthe water systern would
be operated solely by city ernployees. Further, the Commission considered it significant
that the acquisition and operation of a water system is a service commonly provided by
municipalities in their public capacities. Finally, the corporation's bonds enjoyed the
same legal status as those issued by a public body under California's tax and securities
laws.
One year l4ter, the Commission used the same anallical approach to determine
that the Bakersfield Downtown Business Association and Chamber of Commerce were
not "city agencies" that were required to adopt a conflict of interest code. (In re Leach
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. a8.) In Leach, the Commission found that the primary purpose of
the Downtown Business Association and the chamber of commerce was
nongovernmental in character. The Commission stated that "[a]lthough it is true that
both the Association and the Chamberperform certain functions for the City which
presumably are beneficial to the public, we do not think that these activities raise
otherwise private entities to the level of public agencies." (In re Leach, supra.)
TJl,e Siegel and Leach opinions both dealt with determining whether local entities
were public (governmental) or private (non-governmental) in character. lnthe Vonk
opinion, the Commission was faced with a different question - did the Act's conflict of
interest code provisions apply to a statewide agency that was created by the Legislature,
but which functioned similar to aprivate insurance company? Tlte Yonkopinion
addressed whether the State Compensation Insurance Fund was an "agencf' required to
adopt a conflict of interest code under section 87300 of the Act.
Despite the fact that the State Compensation Insurance Fund's functions were
similar to that of a private workers' compensation insurance company, the Commission
concluded that the Fund was a state agency required to adopt a conflict of interest code.
The Vonk Opinion stated:


FileNo. 4-03-182
Page No. 6
"In Siegel and Leach we did isolate a number of specific
criteria which we thought helpful to determine whether
ostensiblyprivate entities were trulypublic in nature.
"These criteria, however, were not intended to be viewed as
constituting a litmus test for determining whether an entity
is public forpurposes of the Political Reform Act. Indeed,
it seems to us that criteria necessary to determine when
private entities become so suffrrsed with attributes of
sovereignty as to be considered public in nature, are simply
not necessary to determine whether an entity specifically
authorized by the state constitution is a public agency. In
the case of the Fund, we believe its constifutional
provenance makes it absolutelyplain that the Fund is
public in nature." (underlining in original)(In re yonk
(1981) 6 FPPC Opinions 1.)
B. Application of the Siegel Criteria.
Following these Commission opinions, we apply the Siegeltest to the facts you
have provided to determine whether BALT should be considered a "local govemment
agenclf'under the Act.
1. Did the impetus for formation of the entity originate with a government
entity?
Generally, the first criterion of the Siegel test is met where an entity is created by
stafute or ordinance or by some official action of another govemment al ageney. (Maas
Advice Letter, No. 4-98-261; Moser Advice Letter,No. 4-97-400.) In tlis case, the
impetus for creating a land trust arose out of Ordinance No. 683, which was adopted by
the City of Brentwood in response to the Agricultural Enterprise Program Final ieport,
produced by a city-formed task force. Ordinance No. 683 expressly frovides that a local
land trust should be formed to acquire and hold agricultural "us"-erris. BALT was
formed on October 24,2002, specifically to implement the Agricultural Enterprise
Program described in Ordinance No. 683 and to work to preserve agriculturalland in the
City of Brentwood and east Contra Costa County.
Because BALT's formation arose out of the adoption of Ordinance No. 683, and
BALT's mission is to implement the progrirm described in the ordinance, the Siegel
formation criterion is met.
2. Is the entity substantially funded by, or is its primary source of funds, a
goYernment agency?
To answer the second question in the siegel analysis, we examine BALT,s
tunding.


File No. A-03-182
Page No. 7
According to the facts you provided, BALT is currently funded by the city from
the mitigation fees paid by developers pursuant to Ordinance No. 683. You anticipate
that BALT will eventually be able to draw on funds from the state Department of
Conservation and federal matching funds to purchase conservation easements.
Because BALT is funded primarily or substantially through govemment funds,
this Siegel criterion is met.
3. 
Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed to
provide services or undertake obligations that public agencies are legally authorized
to perform and which, in fac! they traditionally have performed?
Public agencies have always been legally authoized to restrict the use of land for
the public good. (See sections 6950, et seq.; 51070, et seq. (Open-Space Easement Act of
1974); 51200, et seq. (California Land Conservation Act of 1965 or Williamson Act);
65560, et seq.; Civ. Code $ 815.9) This is essentially what agricultural land trusts do;
they negotiate with landowners to restrict the use of land to agricultural uses in
perpetuity. Indeed, based on research conducted by Commission staff, many land trusts
are operated by public agencies.
Civil Code $ 815.3 provides:
"Only the following entities or orgarizations may acquire
and hold conservation easements:
"(a) Tax-exernpt nonprofit organization qualified under
Section 501(c)(3) of the Intemal Revenue Code and
qualified to do business in this state which has as its
primary purpose the preservation, protection, or
enhancement of land in its nafural, scenic, historical,
agricultural, forested, or open-space condition or use.
"(b) The state or any city, county, city and count5/, district
or other state or local governmental entity, if otherwise
authorized to acquire and hold title to real property and if
the conservation easement is voluntarily conveyed. No
local governmental entity may condition the issuance of an
entitlement for use on the applicant's granting of a
conservation easement pursuant to this chapter."
Although BALT is statutorily authorized to acquire and hold conservation
easements, governmental entities are also authorized to do so and have fulfilled this
function under the above-referenced statutes. Accordingly, the third criterion in the
Stegel analysis is fulfilled.


File No. A-O3-LB2
Page No. 8
d. rs the entity treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions?
BALT was established pursuant to Ordinance 683 and is composed of seven
members. The ordinance requires that three members be appointed by the Brentwood
City Council and three members appointed by the East Contra Costa irrigation Dishict, a
California public agency established pursuant to the Irrigation District Law of the
California Water Code. The seventh director is appointed by the other six. As a non-
profit public benefit corporation, BALT enJoys certain tax benefits similar to other public
agencies. You have concluded that the meetings of BALT's board of directors are
subject to the Brown Act. Consistent with prior advice, BALT's compliance with the
open meeting laws and these other factors means that BALT is a local government
agency under the Siegel analysis. (Cool Advice Letter,No. A-99-084; LoberAdvice
Letter, No. A-90-135.)
Ouestion 2. If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, is it
required to adopt and implement its own conflict of interest code, or
should it comply with the conflict of interest code of another agency?
Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt a conflict ofinterest code
pursuant to the provisions of the Act. Specifically, section 87300 of the Act states that
"[e]very agency shall adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code" applicable to its
"designated ernployee[s]." For purposes of section 87300, "agency'' is interpreted to
mean any state or local government agency. (Maas Advice Letter, No. A-98-261.) We
have already determined that BALT is a local govemment agency. Therefore, the only
question is whether BALT must adopt its own conflict of interest code, or whether itmay
be subject to another agency's conflict ofinterest code.
Because the BALT Board of Directors is not comprised solely ofmembers of the
Brentwood City Council and has an employee (i.e. its executive director), BALT cannot
come under the City of Brentwood's conflict of interest code. Section glZOt provides:
"It is the policy of this actthat conflict of Interest codes
shall be formulated at the most decentralized level possible,
but without precluding intra-departrnental review. Any
question of the level of a department which should be
deemed an"agenct'' for purposes of Section g7300 shall be
resolved by the code reviewing body.,,
Since BALT's jurisdiction extends beyond the boundaries of the City of
Brentwood into Contra Costa CountS the board of supervisors is the appropriate code
reviewing body for its conflict of interest code. (Section 8201t(b).) fuisuant to section
87301, as the code reviewing body, the board of supervisors must ultimately resolve any
questions as to the level of a department that should be deemed an agency. (Stamper


)
3
File No. 4-03-182
Page No. 9
You should consult with the board of supervisors
Advice Lette4 No. I-94-105.
r e gar ding thi s determination.
Ouestion 3.
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act and is
governed by the conflict of interest code of another agency, what
public agency is its appropriate filing officer?
Pursuant to section 87500(o), BALT's board of directors and its executive
director would file with the agency or with the code reviewing body, as provided by the
board of supervisors, the code reviewing body.
Ouestion 4.
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, does
the consulting and farm work done by Boardmember Tom Bloomfield
for the property owner with whom the trust is currently negotiating
for a conservation easement give rise to a conflict of interest under the
Act that would preclude Mr. Bloomfield from participating in board
discussions and decisions pertaining to a conservation easement on the
property?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." ($ 810010).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a "financial interest."
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
($ 87103; reg. 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-step analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a given
govemmental decision. (Reg. 18700(b)(1)-(8).) All of the first six steps must be fulfilled
for a conflict of interest to exist.
Stepl-Publicofficial.
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
($$ 87100, 87103; reg. 18700(b)(l).) '?ublic official" is defined as "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency...." ($82048.) A
"'[m]ember' shall include, but is not limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking authority." (Reg. 1S701(a)(1).)
As a member of BALT's board of directors, Mr. Bloomfield is a "public official" for
pu{poses of the Act, (see $$ 82041, 82048) and the conflict of interest rules apply to him.
3 In deterrnining whether to include an agency within its conflict of interest code, other code
reviewing bodies have looked to the number and strength of connectiors between the agency and the code
reviewing body, as well as the nature of the agency.


FileNo. A-03-182
Page No. 10
Step 2 - Making participating in making, or using an official position to influence
governmental decisions.
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way atternpt[s] to use his [or her] official
position to influence a govemmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to
know he [the official] has a financial interest." ($ 87100; reg. 1s700(b)(2).) A public
official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, actingwithin the authority of
his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to
any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agerrcy. (Reg. 18702.1.)
A public official "pxticipates in a governmental decision" when, actingwithin
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the govemmental decision. (Reg. 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision ie for the purpose of influencing,
the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of
his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.3.)
By taking part in discussions, negotiations, decisions or votes, or attempting to
influence any other board member regarding a conservation easement on the property of
the property owner for whom he performed consulting and farm work, Mr. Bloomfield
would be "participating in a governmental decision" forpurposes of the Act.
If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (i.e. is an "87200 filer') and he
or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: (1) immediatelyprior to the discussion of the item, verbally identifr each tlpe of
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest as
discussed in regulation 18702.5(bXl)(B) on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself
or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion andlor vote on the
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5 (copy
enclosed), subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. ($ 87105.) This section will apply to Mr.
Bloomfield only if BALT manages public investments.
Step 3: \Mhat are the public officialts economic interests - 
the possible sources of a
conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:


a
a
a
File No. A-03-182
Page No. 11
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section S7103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds anyposition of management (section s7103(d); regulation 18703.l(b));a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision (section 87 1 03 (c); regulation 1 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 13703.5).
a
You have stated that the property owner is a source of income to Mr. Bloomfield,
because the property owner has paid $500 or more to Mr. Bloomfield within twelve
months prior to the relevant governmental decision.s
Step 4 - Determining whether the public official's economic interest is directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision(s) in question will have a "material
financial effect" on that interest. First, the official must decide whether the economic
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Reg. 18700(bX4).) Having
established the degree of involvement, the official can then identifu the materiality
standard appropriate to the circumstances. (Reg. 18700(bX5).) The official then knows
what financial eflect would be considered "material" under the Act. Finally, the official
must decide whether such a material financial effect is a "reasonably foreseeable"
consequence of the decision(s) at issue. (Reg. 18700(bX6).)
4 An indirect investrnent or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the ofEcial's immediate family, or theiragents own directly,
indtectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Lnmediati family,, is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
5 Mr. Bloomfield most likely has other economic interests in addition to the property owner.
However, this is the only economic information identifiable from the information providid, and the only
economic interest used in this analysis.


File No. A-03-182
Page No. 12
A person (including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts) is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent, initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, or appeal, or similar request. (Reg. l870a.l(aXl).)
Alternatively, if the person is "a namedparty in, or is the subject of the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agerrcy," then the
person is also directly involved. o.A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Reg. 1870U(a)(2).)
Because the property owner must necessarily be aparty to any proceeding
recorded against his property, pursuant to regulation 18704.1(a)Q), the source of income
is directly involved in the governmental decision.
Steps 5 & 6 - What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon your
economic interest will meet this materiality standard?
Knowing the degree to which the economic interest is involved in the board's
decision, the next step is finding the appropriate standard for evaluating the materiality
that is, the importance of the effect of the decision on the economic interest. (Reg.
18700(bX5).) The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether Mr.
Bloomfield has a conflict of interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5) to
decide if a matertal financial effect on one or more of his economic interests is
"reasonably foreseeable" as a result of the decision. (Reg. 18706.) As used here,
"reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Reg. 18706; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered
reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid.)
For directly involved sources of income, any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material. (Reg.
18705.3(a).) Based on these facts, it is reasonably foreseeable that the grant or denial of
a conservation easement would have some financial effect on the property owner, since
any frnancial effect is deemed material where the source of income is directly involved in
the decision.
Steps 7 & 8 - The o'Public Generally" and s6legally Required Participation"
Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. To fit within this exception, the public official must


File No. A-03-182
Page No. 13
demonstrate that the governmental decision affects the public official's economic
interests in a manner that is indistinguishable from the effect on a significant segment of
the public generally. (Reg. 18707(a).) Based on the facts as we know them, this
exception does not appear to apply.
Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legatly required to
participate in the decision. Again, you have not supplied arry facts to suggest that this
exception applies to your situation.
If you have any other questions regarding this maffer, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
.-il.%6
By: HollyB. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
l. 
Are the members of the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust Board of
Directors and its executive director subject to the requiranents of the Act?
2. 
If the Brentwood AgriculturalLatdTrust is subject to the Act, is it
required to adopt and implement its own conflict of interest code, or should it comply

If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act and should

If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, does the
consulting and farm work done by Boardmember Tom Bloomfield for the property owner

lGovemmentCodesectionsSl000-91014. Commissionregulations appeffatTitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

sections.conclusion:
1. 
In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth four criteria to consider in
determining whether an entity should be considered a "local govemment agenct''subject
to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we find that the Brentwood Agricultural Trust
meets all four criteria and, therefore, conclude that it is a local government agency under
section 82041.
2. 
Because we have concluded that the Brentwood Agriculfural Land Trust is a local
government agerrcy subject to the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest code or
to be covered by the code of another agency. Since it covers more than one jurisdiction
(both the City of Brentwood and part of Contra Costa County), the code reviewing body
for the trust is the board of supervisors. The board of supervisors must make the ultimate
determination as to whether the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust must adopt its own
conflict of interest code or be subsumed within the county's code
3. 
Pursuant to section 87500(o), BALT's board of directors and its executive
director would file with the agency or with the code reviewing body, as provided by the
board of supervisors, the code reviewing body.
4. 
Yes. Because the property owner is a source of income equal to or more than
$500 to Mr. Bloomfield within 12 months of a govemmental decision that directly
involves the property owner, Mr. Bloomfield has a conflict of interest and may not
participate in the decision.
sections.facts:
On August 28,2001, a task force formed by the City of Brentwood issued the
Agricultural Enterprise Program Final Report. The report recommended certain steps
that the city could take to preserve agricultural land and promote agricultural enterprise in
and around the city. On the same date, the Brentwood City Council adopted Ordinance
No. 683 implementing the Agricultural Enterprise Program described in the report and
adding certain provisions to the city's municipal code. Pursuant to the ordinance,
developers submitting subdivision applications for discretionary land use entitlements
from the city must provide agricultural land mitigation by either: (i) granting a farmland
conservation easement to a qualifying entity on a one to one land area ratio, or (ii) paying
an in lieu fee to fund the purchase of such an easement. The ordinance also provides thai
a local land trust should be formed to acquire and hold such agricultural conservation
easements.
on october 24,2002, the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust ("BALT') was
formed pursuant to the Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation Law of California. The
Articles of Incorporation were signed by the board of directors. The bylaws of BALT
provide that BALT shall implement the Agricultural Enterprise Program described in the
ordinance and work to preserve agricultural land in the city and east Contra Costa
County. BALT is actively seeking to acquire agricultural conservation easements in east
Contra Costa County. An agricultural conservation easernent is arealproperty interest
recorded against farmland that limits the land to agricultural uses in perpetuity. In
exchange for granting a conservation easement to BALT, a property owner can receive
compensation for the development value of the land and significant tax benefits. It is
anticipated that BALT will accept donated conservation easements and purchase
easements from property owners. The purchase of conservation easements will be
funded through the mitigation fees levied by the city as well as state Department of
Conservation and federal matching funds.
Composition of the BALT Board of Directors. Thereport recommends a method
for appointing the BALT Board of Directors, and that recommendation is reflected in the
bylaws of BALT. Pursuant to Section 3 of the bylaws, BALT has a seven-member board
of directors. Three of the directors are appointed by the city, three of the directors are
appointed by the East Contra Costa Irrigation District ("ECCID") and one director is
appointed by the other six. ECCID is a Califomia public agency established pursuant to
the Irrigation District Law of the Califomia Water Code.' The geographical jurisdiction
of ECCID is greater than the city but within Contra Costa County. ECCID files the Form
700 of its employees and board of directors with the county.
Executive Dtrector of BALT. In June 2003,BALT hired an executive director to
work eighty hours a month. While BALT has its own post office box and pays the city
for any city services it uses, the executive director currently has an office in the
community development departrnent of the city.
In a telephone conversation on October 21,2003, with Commission staff, you
explained the following: the city does not charge BALT for rent on the office space it
occupies, and the city purchased BALT's telephone and computer system. The city,s
Ordinance No. 683 provides that of the $5,500 per acre agricultural mitigation fee ievied
by the city on developers ,20yo of those funds may be used for BALT's administrative
costs. The city holds the mitigation fees collected in two separate bank accounts, one for
conservation easements and one for BALT's administrative expenses. Once each quarter,
BALT submits a check request to the city for one-fourth of BALT's annual operating
expenses. BALT's annual budget is approved by both the BALT Board of Directors and
by the city council. Although no conservation easements have yet been purchased by
BALT, it is anticipated that when such purchases are made by the board, the item will be
placed on the city council agenda to approve the release of the funds from the mitigation
fee account to complete the purchase.
You also confirmed that BALT complies with the open meeting provisions of the
Brown Act.
Potenttal Confltct of Interest. In the spring of 2003,BALT began negotiations
with a local property owner interested in recording an agricultural conservation easement
2 krigation diskicts are state agencies pursuant to Water Code $ 2}57},but are treated as local
government agencies for puqposes of the Act. (See Francis Advice Letter, No. 4-00-23 1.) Because the
ECCID covers only one count5r, the board of supervisors is its code reviewing body. (Section 82011(b).)


FileNo. A-03-182
on his property. Twenty-five acres of the eighty-acre property is cultivated in grapes.
The property owner has told the BALT Board of Directors that he plans to spend iome of
the moneys he obtains from the sale of the conservation easement to plant grapes on
some or all of the remaining 55 acres of the property. However, it is not anticipated that
BALT will condition the purchase of the conservation easement on any particular use of
the purchase price by the property owner. Assuming that the negotiations between BALT
and the property owner continue successfully, it is likely that it will take six to twelve
months before the conservation easement can be recorded against the property.
One of the members of the BALT Board of Directors, whom you identified to
Commission staff as Tom Bloomfield, is a corlmercial gtapeproperty owner. He has
provided consulting and grape farming services to the property owner regarding the
cultivation of the property. Mr. Bloomfield has received income in excess of $500 within
the past twelve months from the property owner as compensation for rendering such
consulting and farming services.
sections.analysis:
Ouestion l. 
Are the members of the Brentwood Agriculfural Land Trust Board of
Directors and its executive director subject to the requirements of the
Act?
A. Applicable Law.
A "local govemment agency''is defined in the Act as "a county, city or district of
any kind including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or
any departrnent, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." (Section 82041.)
The inquiry here is whether BALT is a local government agency. The
Commission used a four-part factual test to distinguish govemmental from non-
governmental entities in In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62. In the Siegel opinion, the
Commission was faced with the question of whether the Pico Rivera Water Development
Corporation, a nonprofit corporation, should be considered a local government entiiy.
The Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation was a nonprofit corporation that was
founded to acquire, maintain, and operate a water system.
To determine whether the Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation was a
govemmental entity, the Commission considered four questions:
(1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity
originated with a govemment agency;
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its
primary source of funds is, a government agency;


FileNo. A-03-182
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the
entity was formed is to provide services or undertake
obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to
perform and which, infact, they traditionallyhave
performed; and
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other
statutory provisions.
The Commission found that the city council was directly involved in the
formation of the water development corporation, and that the city council had the right to
disapprove the name of anyone submitted to serve on the board. With respect to funding,
it found that the city was required to pay rent to the corporation until the bonds were
retired, even if receipts from the operation of the water system were not sufficient to meet
these costs - in essence, guaranteeing the bonds of the corporation. More evidence that
the corporation was fulfilling a public function was the factthatthe water systern would
be operated solely by city ernployees. Further, the Commission considered it significant
that the acquisition and operation of a water system is a service commonly provided by
municipalities in their public capacities. Finally, the corporation's bonds enjoyed the
same legal status as those issued by a public body under California's tax and securities
laws.
One year l4ter, the Commission used the same anallical approach to determine
that the Bakersfield Downtown Business Association and Chamber of Commerce were
not "city agencies" that were required to adopt a conflict of interest code. (In re Leach
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. a8.) In Leach, the Commission found that the primary purpose of
the Downtown Business Association and the chamber of commerce was
nongovernmental in character. The Commission stated that "[a]lthough it is true that
both the Association and the Chamberperform certain functions for the City which
presumably are beneficial to the public, we do not think that these activities raise
otherwise private entities to the level of public agencies." (In re Leach, supra.)
TJl,e Siegel and Leach opinions both dealt with determining whether local entities
were public (governmental) or private (non-governmental) in character. lnthe Vonk
opinion, the Commission was faced with a different question - did the Act's conflict of
interest code provisions apply to a statewide agency that was created by the Legislature,
but which functioned similar to aprivate insurance company? Tlte Yonkopinion
addressed whether the State Compensation Insurance Fund was an "agencf' required to
adopt a conflict of interest code under section 87300 of the Act.
Despite the fact that the State Compensation Insurance Fund's functions were
similar to that of a private workers' compensation insurance company, the Commission
concluded that the Fund was a state agency required to adopt a conflict of interest code.
The Vonk Opinion stated:


FileNo. 4-03-182
"In Siegel and Leach we did isolate a number of specific
criteria which we thought helpful to determine whether
ostensiblyprivate entities were trulypublic in nature.
"These criteria, however, were not intended to be viewed as
constituting a litmus test for determining whether an entity
is public forpurposes of the Political Reform Act. Indeed,
it seems to us that criteria necessary to determine when
private entities become so suffrrsed with attributes of
sovereignty as to be considered public in nature, are simply
not necessary to determine whether an entity specifically
authorized by the state constitution is a public agency. In
the case of the Fund, we believe its constifutional
provenance makes it absolutelyplain that the Fund is
public in nature." (underlining in original)(In re yonk
(1981) 6 FPPC Opinions 1.)
B. Application of the Siegel Criteria.
Following these Commission opinions, we apply the Siegeltest to the facts you
have provided to determine whether BALT should be considered a "local govemment
agenclf'under the Act.
1. Did the impetus for formation of the entity originate with a government
entity?
Generally, the first criterion of the Siegel test is met where an entity is created by
stafute or ordinance or by some official action of another govemment al ageney. (Maas
Advice Letter, No. 4-98-261; Moser Advice Letter,No. 4-97-400.) In tlis case, the
impetus for creating a land trust arose out of Ordinance No. 683, which was adopted by
the City of Brentwood in response to the Agricultural Enterprise Program Final ieport,
produced by a city-formed task force. Ordinance No. 683 expressly frovides that a local
land trust should be formed to acquire and hold agricultural "us"-erris. BALT was
formed on October 24,2002, specifically to implement the Agricultural Enterprise
Program described in Ordinance No. 683 and to work to preserve agriculturalland in the
City of Brentwood and east Contra Costa County.
Because BALT's formation arose out of the adoption of Ordinance No. 683, and
BALT's mission is to implement the progrirm described in the ordinance, the Siegel
formation criterion is met.
2. Is the entity substantially funded by, or is its primary source of funds, a
goYernment agency?
To answer the second question in the siegel analysis, we examine BALT,s
tunding.
According to the facts you provided, BALT is currently funded by the city from
the mitigation fees paid by developers pursuant to Ordinance No. 683. You anticipate
that BALT will eventually be able to draw on funds from the state Department of
Conservation and federal matching funds to purchase conservation easements.
Because BALT is funded primarily or substantially through govemment funds,
this Siegel criterion is met.
3. 
Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed to
provide services or undertake obligations that public agencies are legally authorized
to perform and which, in fac! they traditionally have performed?
Public agencies have always been legally authoized to restrict the use of land for
the public good. (See sections 6950, et seq.; 51070, et seq. (Open-Space Easement Act of
1974); 51200, et seq. (California Land Conservation Act of 1965 or Williamson Act);
65560, et seq.; Civ. Code $ 815.9) This is essentially what agricultural land trusts do;
they negotiate with landowners to restrict the use of land to agricultural uses in
perpetuity. Indeed, based on research conducted by Commission staff, many land trusts
are operated by public agencies.
Civil Code $ 815.3 provides:
"Only the following entities or orgarizations may acquire
and hold conservation easements:
"(a) Tax-exernpt nonprofit organization qualified under
Section 501(c)(3) of the Intemal Revenue Code and
qualified to do business in this state which has as its
primary purpose the preservation, protection, or
enhancement of land in its nafural, scenic, historical,
agricultural, forested, or open-space condition or use.
"(b) The state or any city, county, city and count5/, district
or other state or local governmental entity, if otherwise
authorized to acquire and hold title to real property and if
the conservation easement is voluntarily conveyed. No
local governmental entity may condition the issuance of an
entitlement for use on the applicant's granting of a
conservation easement pursuant to this chapter."
Although BALT is statutorily authorized to acquire and hold conservation
easements, governmental entities are also authorized to do so and have fulfilled this
function under the above-referenced statutes. Accordingly, the third criterion in the
Stegel analysis is fulfilled.


File No. A-O3-LB2
d. rs the entity treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions?
BALT was established pursuant to Ordinance 683 and is composed of seven
members. The ordinance requires that three members be appointed by the Brentwood
City Council and three members appointed by the East Contra Costa irrigation Dishict, a
California public agency established pursuant to the Irrigation District Law of the
California Water Code. The seventh director is appointed by the other six. As a non-
profit public benefit corporation, BALT enJoys certain tax benefits similar to other public
agencies. You have concluded that the meetings of BALT's board of directors are
subject to the Brown Act. Consistent with prior advice, BALT's compliance with the
open meeting laws and these other factors means that BALT is a local government
agency under the Siegel analysis. (Cool Advice Letter,No. A-99-084; LoberAdvice
Letter, No. A-90-135.)
Ouestion 2. If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, is it
required to adopt and implement its own conflict of interest code, or
should it comply with the conflict of interest code of another agency?
Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt a conflict ofinterest code
pursuant to the provisions of the Act. Specifically, section 87300 of the Act states that
"[e]very agency shall adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code" applicable to its
"designated ernployee[s]." For purposes of section 87300, "agency'' is interpreted to
mean any state or local government agency. (Maas Advice Letter, No. A-98-261.) We
have already determined that BALT is a local govemment agency. Therefore, the only
question is whether BALT must adopt its own conflict of interest code, or whether itmay
be subject to another agency's conflict ofinterest code.
Because the BALT Board of Directors is not comprised solely ofmembers of the
Brentwood City Council and has an employee (i.e. its executive director), BALT cannot
come under the City of Brentwood's conflict of interest code. Section glZOt provides:
"It is the policy of this actthat conflict of Interest codes
shall be formulated at the most decentralized level possible,
but without precluding intra-departrnental review. Any
question of the level of a department which should be
deemed an"agenct'' for purposes of Section g7300 shall be
resolved by the code reviewing body.,,
Since BALT's jurisdiction extends beyond the boundaries of the City of
Brentwood into Contra Costa CountS the board of supervisors is the appropriate code
reviewing body for its conflict of interest code. (Section 8201t(b).) fuisuant to section
87301, as the code reviewing body, the board of supervisors must ultimately resolve any
questions as to the level of a department that should be deemed an agency. (Stamper


)
3
You should consult with the board of supervisors
Advice Lette4 No. I-94-105.
r e gar ding thi s determination.
Ouestion 3.
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act and is
governed by the conflict of interest code of another agency, what
public agency is its appropriate filing officer?
Pursuant to section 87500(o), BALT's board of directors and its executive
director would file with the agency or with the code reviewing body, as provided by the
board of supervisors, the code reviewing body.
Ouestion 4.
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, does
the consulting and farm work done by Boardmember Tom Bloomfield
for the property owner with whom the trust is currently negotiating
for a conservation easement give rise to a conflict of interest under the
Act that would preclude Mr. Bloomfield from participating in board
discussions and decisions pertaining to a conservation easement on the
property?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." ($ 810010).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a "financial interest."
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
($ 87103; reg. 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-step analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a given
govemmental decision. (Reg. 18700(b)(1)-(8).) All of the first six steps must be fulfilled
for a conflict of interest to exist.
Stepl-Publicofficial.
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
($$ 87100, 87103; reg. 18700(b)(l).) '?ublic official" is defined as "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency...." ($82048.) A
"'[m]ember' shall include, but is not limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking authority." (Reg. 1S701(a)(1).)
As a member of BALT's board of directors, Mr. Bloomfield is a "public official" for
pu{poses of the Act, (see $$ 82041, 82048) and the conflict of interest rules apply to him.
3 In deterrnining whether to include an agency within its conflict of interest code, other code
reviewing bodies have looked to the number and strength of connectiors between the agency and the code
reviewing body, as well as the nature of the agency.


FileNo. A-03-182
Step 2 - Making participating in making, or using an official position to influence
governmental decisions.
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way atternpt[s] to use his [or her] official
position to influence a govemmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to
know he [the official] has a financial interest." ($ 87100; reg. 1s700(b)(2).) A public
official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, actingwithin the authority of
his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to
any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agerrcy. (Reg. 18702.1.)
A public official "pxticipates in a governmental decision" when, actingwithin
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the govemmental decision. (Reg. 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision ie for the purpose of influencing,
the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of
his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.3.)
By taking part in discussions, negotiations, decisions or votes, or attempting to
influence any other board member regarding a conservation easement on the property of
the property owner for whom he performed consulting and farm work, Mr. Bloomfield
would be "participating in a governmental decision" forpurposes of the Act.
If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (i.e. is an "87200 filer') and he
or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: (1) immediatelyprior to the discussion of the item, verbally identifr each tlpe of
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest as
discussed in regulation 18702.5(bXl)(B) on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself
or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion andlor vote on the
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5 (copy
enclosed), subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. ($ 87105.) This section will apply to Mr.
Bloomfield only if BALT manages public investments.
Step 3: \Mhat are the public officialts economic interests - 
the possible sources of a
conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:


a
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section S7103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds anyposition of management (section s7103(d); regulation 18703.l(b));a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision (section 87 1 03 (c); regulation 1 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 13703.5).
a
You have stated that the property owner is a source of income to Mr. Bloomfield,
because the property owner has paid $500 or more to Mr. Bloomfield within twelve
months prior to the relevant governmental decision.s
Step 4 - Determining whether the public official's economic interest is directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision(s) in question will have a "material
financial effect" on that interest. First, the official must decide whether the economic
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Reg. 18700(bX4).) Having
established the degree of involvement, the official can then identifu the materiality
standard appropriate to the circumstances. (Reg. 18700(bX5).) The official then knows
what financial eflect would be considered "material" under the Act. Finally, the official
must decide whether such a material financial effect is a "reasonably foreseeable"
consequence of the decision(s) at issue. (Reg. 18700(bX6).)
4 An indirect investrnent or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the ofEcial's immediate family, or theiragents own directly,
indtectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Lnmediati family,, is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
5 Mr. Bloomfield most likely has other economic interests in addition to the property owner.
However, this is the only economic information identifiable from the information providid, and the only
economic interest used in this analysis.
A person (including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts) is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent, initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, or appeal, or similar request. (Reg. l870a.l(aXl).)
Alternatively, if the person is "a namedparty in, or is the subject of the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agerrcy," then the
person is also directly involved. o.A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Reg. 1870U(a)(2).)
Because the property owner must necessarily be aparty to any proceeding
recorded against his property, pursuant to regulation 18704.1(a)Q), the source of income
is directly involved in the governmental decision.
Steps 5 & 6 - What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon your
economic interest will meet this materiality standard?
Knowing the degree to which the economic interest is involved in the board's
decision, the next step is finding the appropriate standard for evaluating the materiality
that is, the importance of the effect of the decision on the economic interest. (Reg.
18700(bX5).) The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether Mr.
Bloomfield has a conflict of interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5) to
decide if a matertal financial effect on one or more of his economic interests is
"reasonably foreseeable" as a result of the decision. (Reg. 18706.) As used here,
"reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Reg. 18706; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered
reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid.)
For directly involved sources of income, any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material. (Reg.
18705.3(a).) Based on these facts, it is reasonably foreseeable that the grant or denial of
a conservation easement would have some financial effect on the property owner, since
any frnancial effect is deemed material where the source of income is directly involved in
the decision.
Steps 7 & 8 - The o'Public Generally" and s6legally Required Participation"
Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. To fit within this exception, the public official must
demonstrate that the governmental decision affects the public official's economic
interests in a manner that is indistinguishable from the effect on a significant segment of
the public generally. (Reg. 18707(a).) Based on the facts as we know them, this
exception does not appear to apply.
Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legatly required to
participate in the decision. Again, you have not supplied arry facts to suggest that this
exception applies to your situation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: l. 
Are the members of the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust Board of
Directors and its executive director subject to the requiranents of the Act?
2. 
If the Brentwood AgriculturalLatdTrust is subject to the Act, is it
required to adopt and implement its own conflict of interest code, or should it comply
with the conflict of interest code of another agency?
3. 
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act and should
comply under the conflict of interest code of another agency, what public agency is its
appropriate fi ling offi cer?
4. 
If the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust is subject to the Act, does the
consulting and farm work done by Boardmember Tom Bloomfield for the property owner
with whom the trust is currently negotiating for a conservation easement give rise to a
conflict of interest under the Act that would preclude Mr. Bloomfield from participating
in board discussions and decisions pertaining to a conservation easement on the property?
lGovemmentCodesectionsSl000-91014. Commissionregulations appeffatTitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
-ua-dl


FileNo. A-03-182

CONCLUSION: 1. 
In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth four criteria to consider in
determining whether an entity should be considered a "local govemment agenct''subject
to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we find that the Brentwood Agricultural Trust
meets all four criteria and, therefore, conclude that it is a local government agency under
section 82041.
2. 
Because we have concluded that the Brentwood Agriculfural Land Trust is a local
government agerrcy subject to the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest code or
to be covered by the code of another agency. Since it covers more than one jurisdiction
(both the City of Brentwood and part of Contra Costa County), the code reviewing body
for the trust is the board of supervisors. The board of supervisors must make the ultimate
determination as to whether the Brentwood Agricultural Land Trust must adopt its own
conflict of interest code or be subsumed within the county's code
3. 
Pursuant to section 87500(o), BALT's board of directors and its executive
director would file with the agency or with the code reviewing body, as provided by the
board of supervisors, the code reviewing body.
4. 
Yes. Because the property owner is a source of income equal to or more than
$500 to Mr. Bloomfield within 12 months of a govemmental decision that directly
involves the property owner, Mr. Bloomfield has a conflict of interest and may not
participate in the decision.
